turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: We will hear arguments first this morning in Anderson Bros. v. Valencia. Mr. Kramer, you may proceed whenever you are ready.

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: Thank you, Your Honor. Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: This case is here on certiorari from the Seventh Circuit Court of Appeals, and it poses two broad issues. try, whose stock in trade is security interests failed to recognize and disclose a security interest required to be disclosed under the Truth-in-Lending Act. Secondly, this case poses the question of whether even if such a hard to imagine mistake occurred, the ends of justice are served by applying any such decision retroactively to any case other than the one at bar.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Will these issues all disappear under the new statute that was passed last year?

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: They will, Your Honor, but not with respect to contracts that were written with respect to the

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And what is the volume of those con­ tracts , have you any idea?

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: Well there are -- this contract that is before the Court is the paradigm of the industries contract It has --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well are we talking about millions of contracts?

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: We are talking about millions of contracts, Your Honor. This is the industries' form --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How many cases are pending? the precise number of cases which have raised this issue. There have been, however, 27 decisions of various circuit and district courts on this issue in 15 different cases that have already reached the courts. Certainly whatever the decision of this Court is on this issue will be the landmark case in this area. And depending on what this Court holds --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well landmark, except for the new statute.

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: Except for the new statute, Your Honor

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Which will remove the problem for the future ? statute there's a fixed recovery, is there not, $1000 or something?

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: Well Your Honor, for certain vio­ lations --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: For this one.

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: For this one it is twice the amount -- penalty in -- twice the amount of the finance charge, up to $1,000 per contract where the Truth-in-Lending Act is violated. And what the Court has before it now is the industries' form --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What you're suggesting is that .affirm­ ance here would encourage lawsuits to recover that penalty, is that right?

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: I am most certainly saying that, Your Honor. As well, an affirmance here would put defaulting debtors in the position of having, even those that are -- have a contract that was entered into more than a year prior to the statute of limitations would have --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is. there any limitation of jurisdic­ tion of the federal courts for such suits?

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: No there is not, Your Honor. These actions may be brought in either the federal courts or the state courts. But most jurisdictions have state laws which provide that even time barred counter-claims for contracts entered into for more than a year prior to the date on which any defaulting debtor who is sued by a creditor to collect a entered into his contract four or five years ago could bring a $1000 plus attorneys fees counter-claim, or action for recoupment under most jurisdictions and that would allegations. In particular, it dismissed the allegation as which was an assignment of physical damage in unearned in­ interest, that was the allegation, and that was one of the five allegations which was entirely dismissed by the trial court. As to this particular allegation that the assign­ ment was a security interest that should have been not disclosed on the back but on the front of the contract under the Truth-in-Lending Act, but the District Court looked at the normal meaning of the term security interest in the Act, and looked at the usual and customary meaning of those words as used in the contract, and found that there was no security interest here in the assignment of unearned physical damage insurance premiums, appearing on the back of the contract. And therefore, no requirement that that appear on the front of the contract. Now, developments occurred in the Fifth Circuit where the Fifth Circuit rendered an opinion contrary to that which we called to the attention of the District Court and he reversed his position, eventually that reversal against finding the violation of the Truth-in-Lending Act as to this assignment clause was affirmed by the Seventh Circuit. There are four uncontrovertible elements which stand as walls that surround the issues in this case, and we respectfully submit that the Court's decision will be made somewhere within the confines of those four walls. They are: the true nature of the interest, the assignment in­ terest that is before this Court; the purpose of the Act and the specific legislative history of adoption of the Act's security interest provision; the promulgation by the Federal Reserve Board staff in 1981, of -- Federal Reserve Board Proposed Official Interpretation 173, which is precisely in point on this issue.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Only? It has nothing to do with the this issue, the Federal Reserve Board also had the benefit of those conflicting decisions, all before it held in its proposed -- and it has not yet been adopted and it was not finalized --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Now what was the gist of 173?

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: The gist of 173, Your Honor, was that consumers are not aided by the disclosure of the assignment clause of the physical damage insurance -- the insurance premiums on the back of the contract -- they would not be out the purpose --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But it wasn't explicit,, was it, that the assignment was not a security interest?

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: They held that although -- a technical reading, as had been accomplished in certain courts in cer­ tain Circuit Courts could support that decision that that would -- that such an incidental interest was not intended by the Act, and that the purposes of the Act are better served by not disclosing that provision as a security inter­ est on the front.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, but yes or no -- did they interpret a bulletin to say that the assignment was not a security interest for purposes of the statute?

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: For purposes of the statute they said it should not be disclosed as a security interest, yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Kramer, I have some difficulty with the various levels of regulations and the weight we should give them. The 1970 regulation, which doesn't seem to be mentioned in the 1981 promulgation, simply, at the end, says in the event of the customers default, your client would have the right to cancel the policy and apply any premium refund . to the unpaid balance of the loan. Under the circumstances, we think it would be appropriate to disclose interest under 12 C.F.R. Section so-and-so. I had some trouble with that, originally, because it didn't seem to me to state flatly it must be disclosed, the words it is appro­ priate to disclose it simply said may be in an excess of caution -- but I was also troubled by the fact that the 1981 promulgation, the new regulation which goes in exactly the opposite direction, didn't seem to refer to the 1970 regulation.

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: Your Honor, I don't believe that they were exactly the same. The 1970 unofficial interpretation issued by the Board staff does certainly use the word appro­ priate. However, it involved a different kind of insurance than physical damage insurance on a vehicle, where the vehicle is the real security interest in the transaction. And the only security interest which appears in the 1970 unofficial interpretation by the Board was the lender's security interest in the insurance itself, which he was financing; it is not at all the same as the transaction where the unearned insur­ ance premiums on physical damage insurance are entirely incidental to the real security interest in the transaction as we have here, which is the financed vehicle, which is clearly disclosed on the front of the contract and it's disclosure would be clouded if there were such an incidental interest taken off of the back of the industries' form con­ security interest the consumer was giving up in the trans­ action .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: When you say they were different kinds of insurance, the 1970 letter referred to life insurance?

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: Accidental death and dismemberment policy where there was a single lifetime premium, Your Honor, and the only thing that the creditor was taking in that case was a security interest in the unearned premiums. There was no other security interest in that transaction.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And the 1981 regulation would apply to unearned premiums on physical damage -- insurance.

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: Physical damage insurance on a vehicle which is exactly what we have here.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Why hasn't the '81 proposal been finalized? There just isn't time, or is it --

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: No, Your Honor, it's very clear that it is out of deference for this Court's authority. Clearly, the Federal Reserve Board under the Court's opinion in Milhollin had the authority to clear up this matter once and for all, and tell the credit community which is only looking for direction in such matters, and will follow that direction from a central authority -- which Your Honors found in Milhollin was the Federal Reserve Board clearly, and its power was to enforce, apply and interpret the Act and the regulation. That -- what the Board has referred to as a deferral of final action, and not a withdrawal at all, came only after this Court granted certiorari in this case. And in light of the fact that the 172 prior offic­ ial staff interpretations of the Federal Reserve Board have been adopted without any substantive change, as we indi­ cated in Appendix B to our reply brief, is a factor that the Court ought to consider, because these things were not promul­ gated - as trial balloons, as counsel for the Respondents has argued, but rather as seriously well considered and thoroughly thought-through determinations by the Federal Reserve Board as to interpreting the Act that governs the framework of, in determining commerce and credit in the United States . Indeed, although 173 is only a proposed agency interpretation, the Courts have held applying this Court's Skidmore v. Swift decision, that even a proposed agency interpretation is entitled to such persuasive weight as is evidenced from such things as the thoroughness of its con­ sideration and the validity of its reasoning. Petitioners submit --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I suppose that's true only with respect to agencies which are not empowered to, in effect, Kind of fill in the blanks or make definitions and that sort of thing? Skidmore v. Swift dealt with an agency which was not expressly authorized by Congress to interpret the Act --

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, but the Federal Reserve Board, of course, is empowered to interpret the Act, just as the Securities and Exchange Commission is empowered to interpret the Securities laws. And this Court's opinion in 1975, the Court held that even though the -- in the Forman case, the securities law of 1934 refers to any stock -- the Securities and Exchange Commission had the power to inter­ pret that to exclude stock in a cooperative housing project as not being a security, even though the express words of the language of the statute that the agency was empowered to interpret did use the term any stock, the Respondents in this case, make an argument to the effect that the strict words of the statute should be followed in this case, or the strict words of the regulation, which indeed are not in point premiums, but the Respondent's literal interpretation cuts the heart of the purpose and intent of the Act out of it, does not look at the purpose of the Act, which is to permit informed, comparison credit shopping as this Court held in the Milhollin decision. And the Board's determination, after having thoroughly considered, in proposing 173, the 27 decisions in the conflicting district and circuit court cases on this matter, who had -- the Board had, at the time they proposed 173, the benefit of intensive Congressional revision of Regulation Z before it proposed 173, so that it is the position of the Petitioners given those factors the determination of the Federal Reserve Board particularly

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And if that disclosure had been put on the front, would that -- is it agreed that it would have been adequate?

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: Well Your Honor, there is a problem with respect to that, because the Courts have held, includ­ ing our Seventh Circuit, has held that there is a violation of the Truth-in-Lending Act if you overdisclose and claim a security interest where you do not have one. For example

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I know. But in this particular case if the disclosure on the back had been put on the front

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: I don't think the Seventh Circuit would have come out the way it did, Your Honor. I think --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It would have -- there would have been no problem then, I suppose?

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: That's right. The point of the Peti­ tioners is that the Seventh Circuit was wrong in its decision And indeed, in reading its decision and in reading the very reluctant, two concurring opinions, from Judges Cudahy and Swygert, it is clear that the Seventh Circuit felt it was being dragged along by what it was being required to do in providing a very liberal and very technical construction of the Truth-in-Lending Act. And I would point out to -- source of the Seventh Circuit's decision that the disclosure

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Did they say it violated the statute?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And, wholly aside from Regulation Z?

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: They said, the cases that have so held have held that it's a violation of the statute and the regulation. However, that rationale would put everything that was on the back of the contract on the front of the contract, Your Honor. It would not, as the Board has noted in 173., promote proper -- the purposes of the act or proper comparison credit shopping --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well what if -- what is your position if it hadn't been disclosed at all?

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: If it hadn't been disclosed at all, even on the back, Your Honor, then the Creditor would not have that right to unearned physical damage insurance premiums. It would not be -- part of the contract.

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: It would not then be part of the contract if you couldn't claim it. Putting it on the back makes it part of the contract; the issue in this case is whether it was required under the Truth-in-Lending Act to be disclosed on the front as a security interest, along with the vehicle which was the real security interest in this transaction.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Kramer, just to be sure I'm right,

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- it's this language, is it, that appeal on the back: "buyer hereby assigns to seller any monies payable under such insurance, by whomever obtained, including returned or unearned premiums."

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And did you answer my brother White that if that had appeared on the front it would have -- it w ould not have been adequate?

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: It would have satisfied the Seventh Circuit, Your Honor, but I would point out two important factors with respect to that. The first of which is, 173 was not available to the Seventh Circuit when it wrote its decision. If Your Honor looks at footnote 24, I believe it is, in the Seventh Circuit's decision, it is obvious, and that footnote states, "it may be critical to the credit industry that there is not an official staff interpretation of the Federal Reserve Board on this question, but there is not."

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: There still isn't.

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: And -- and there still isn't, Your Honor. Except, but for -- but for this Court's grant of certiorari, it --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: One-seventy-three would be operative.

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: It would be operative, it would be operative. And as I --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But I still don't -- without regard to view whether had what I read you appeared on the front, would that have satisfied any disclosure requirement -- do you suggest?

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: Your Honor, it would not have satisfied a requirement of the statute or the regulation, because neither the regulation nor the statute require it, and all that must be on the front is what is required of it, and as I've indicated --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I see.

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: -- one of the problems faced by creditors in this field is that if they put on the front of such a contract something which is -- the state --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, but if this -- if we were to say that this is a security interest -- not assignment --

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- then, I gather, under the statute and regulation, irrespective of 173 would have had to have appeared on the front, would it?

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: If Your.Honors so state, that's correct Indeed, if Your Honors were to so hold --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well I thought your suggestion was, and 173's suggestion was that even if this is a security inter­ est, it isn't the kind that needs to appear on the front?

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: It is the -- 173 does not state, cate­ 173 says is that this incidental interest --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Even if it is, it needn't appear on the front?

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: It's not the type of interest that need appear on the front, or --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Isn't it awfully hard for you to suggest that this isn't a security interest, if you have the right to -- you agree that if it weren't either on the front or the back you wouldn't have it?

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: I would agree, Your Honor, that if it weren't on the back we wouldn't have the right. I don't agree that it is a security interest, it does not have the elements that a security interest classically has.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But 173 is to the effect that even if it is it needn't be on the front?

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: To the effect, Your Honor, that the -- it doesn't use those exact words, it says this incidental interest need not be shown on the front, it would not aid the purposes of the Act, in fact, it would retard the purposes of the Act if it were shown on the front. It would confuse,-- in the Milhollin case -- I had the opportunity to read the argument that was made before the Court in that case. And in that case, the argument was made that simply by adding two words to the front of the same contract there could be compliance with the Truth-in-Lending Act. Respondents in this case allege that by adding five words to the front of this contract there could be compliance with the Act--

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Kramer, may I be. sure about this? MR.KRAMER: Yes, Mr. Justice?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The reason, if it has to appear on the front, is because the statute says security interests must be disclosed on the front and if this is a security interest that provision of the statute then requires its appearance on the front.

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: Exactly. And this is our position and the Federal Reserve Board's proposed position is not such a required Truth-in-Lending security interest required under the Act to appear on the front.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Kramer, I think a moment ago you closure must be on the front?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: All right. So what does it -- MR. KRAMER Regulation Z would be 12 C.F.R. --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What page of the petition? MR. KRAMER We've listed these in the front, Your Honor -- on page -- pages 1 and 2. I would point out --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well I still want to know where are the words that say a disclosure must be on the front? MR. KRAMER The requirement, Your Honor, would be with reference to disclosure statements under the Truth- in-Lending Act. The regulation and the statute uses the term disclosure statement, and for purposes of the Act the front of the contract that's in Court now is the provided that the entire contract should be the disclosure

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: That would not be permissible under In conclusion, we have argued in our brief and of the original Truth-in-Lending Act, the Act that this case comes under, and its security interest provisions, came from the floor of Congress with the idea of preventing not -- and not affecting the type of interest which is before the Court now, but what was referred to on the floor of Congress by Congressman Cahill and Senator Proxmire, as a vicious second mortgage racket that involved the taking of second mortgages on purchasers homes and borrowers homes, unwittingly given by such borrowers -- that's what the security interest provision of the Act was adopted -- to put in -- that's how it came up from the floor of Congress. The incidental interest which is before the Court Congress had in mind when it originally adopted the Act, un­ In conclusion, I would cite to the Court, --

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: No, Your Honor, we will not have to make any disclosure with respect to incidental interests, such as --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I'm talking about the security interest in the automobile?

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: In the automobile, we will, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But that's the one you did make in the contract?

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: That's the one we did make, and --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That was not a second mortgage?

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: And that was not a second mortgage.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So that the security interest require­ ment is not limited to second mortgages?

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: It is limited -- Congress' intent it's not -- the property is not significant, as I understand

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That insurance is rather a sort of -- a fringe --

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: That's correct. As Judge Cudahy said, and he was joined in by Judge Swygert in his concur­ ring opinion, that to require the disclosure of this par­ ticular assignment on the face of the contract would merely add virtually inconsequential information; lengthening, complicating and trivializing this disclosure for no apparent reason.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But you said earlier, I think you were interrupted, that only five words would have to be added to this phrase.

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: That's Respondent's contention, and this Court rejected the contention in Milhollin with only two words having to be added to that contract, because it of whether it's true or not, is it not true that by just adding the words "and insurance policies", or something like that, you would have an adequate disclosure?

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: It could be done, Your Honor. It Is done on a new form of:. Ford Credit Contract, however, that form of contract gives up significant interest that is taken under this broader form. For the reasons that we've stated in our briefs and that I've had a chance to reach in or at the minimum, because of the problems created with respect to this form contract, make any decision on this issue affirming prospective only. Thank you.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Mr. Alop.

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Mr. Alop, forgive me, before you start, but where is it -- that the requirement that it appear on the face -- where is that in the statute?

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: That's in Regulation Z, Your Honor, at 12 C.F.R. Section 226.8(a).

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Now here's Regulation Z at page 2 of the customer's signature. Where the customer signs on the front side of the page and only on the front side of the page, all disclosures must be --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What are you reading from?

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: I'm not reading from the brief, Your Honor. The Regulation Z quotation does not appear in either of the briefs.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Where do we find it?

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: Twelve, Code of Federal Regulations --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Isn't that the crux of this case?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, well, there's no controversy about it, is there?

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: The other side has not disputed this. The Petitioners have never claimed that it could be ade­ quately disclosed on the reverse side of the contract.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, but it wouldn't -- it would only be because of the regulation?

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor. That's at 12 C.F.R. -- The use of the broad language "any security interest" reflects an unmistakable intention of Congress that every security interest retained by a creditor in the course of a credit transaction be disclosed. This Court, in the cases of Shea v. Vialpando and Harrison v. PPG Industries has indicated that the use of the term "any" precludes limited or narrowed statutory construction. The broad language in the statute, "any security interest", would also controvert -- Ford Credit's suggestion, that only essential security interests need be disclosed.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well now, the Proposed Official Staff Interpretation 173, which as I understand it has not yet become effective, does say that this kind of interest need not be -- need not appear on the face of the statement. And if it says that, doesn't it necessarily also say that it's not a security interest, if the statute, as you tell us unequivocally requires that any security interests do appear?

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: I would not dispute that, Your Honor. The proposed FC-0173 does leave one with the implication that it is not a security interest.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Where does the relevant text to that regulation appear?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: I believe it's in the -- page 33 of the addendum -- no, 54 of the addendum.

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: To the Petitionters' brief. It's addendum number 54.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What color?

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: It's a blue brief.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, let me ask you, you read 228.8(a) is that it, a moment ago?

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And that's the source of the require­ ment for the disclosure being on the front?

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Now, what if in that regulation it had said except security interests in the unearned premium?

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: If that --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And then those may be on the back? Suppose that that regulation said that?

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: Then we would not have this lawsuit before the Court today, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well why isn't that -- if 173 is adopted, why isn't that in effect an amendment of -- it just adds an exception to 228(a).

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: The point is that EC-0173 is an unadopted, mere proposal of the Federal Reserve Board that was issued with the caveat that it may be withdrawn or that it may be altered after public comment was scrutinized. in writing specifically precluded any reliance on FC-0173 and it has deferred any final action on FC-0173 as a result of this Court's granting of certiorari in this case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you concede that if the Federal Reserve Board goes ahead and adopts the resolution, it will be a valid regulation?

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: If the Federal Reserve Board adopted FC-0173 because it uses the test of incidentalness which is at variance with the plain language of the Truth-in-Lending Act and Regulation Z, and because it is also at variance with longstanding Federal Reserve Board Regulations, or rather, the interpretation in Public Information Letter 377, it would still be entitled to little deference under the traditional test of Skidmore v. Swift S Co.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But Regulation Z is, itself, a promul­ gation of the Federal Reserve Board, is it not?

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, to say then that it's contrary to Regulation Z, when the Regulation Z is in effect super­ seded in part by this new regulation, is rather hard for me to follow.

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: I also said that it is at variance with the language of the Act, which requires --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That was not hard to follow. But do you really place much reliance on the fact that the new regulation is at variance with the old regulation?

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: I place a reliance on the fact that Regulation Z defines security interest to encompass any interest in property which secures payment and that this proposal uses the test of incidentalness which ignores the concept in Regulation Z that any interest in property con­ stitutes a security interest.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well what if this proposal had been promulgated right after passage of the Act? Because the Act doesn't contain, as I understand it, any definition of the phrase "security interest".

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: The Act does not, Your Honor; it is Regulation Z --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And what if 0173 had been promulgated, immediately after passage of the Act, saying security interests means any -- does not include an incidental security.

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: Had that been the case, I believe there would have been a contradiction between the Regulation Z definition --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, there would have been no Regula­ tion Z. Regulation Z, itself, would have, under my hypothesis would have included the present provisions of 0173.

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: Given that hypothesis -- that Section Regulation Z would not exist, then of course FC-0173, if it was final action, would control. But given --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It would control, because the statute -- while it does require that a description of any security interests be stated, it doesn't define what a security interest is, does it?

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: No, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And it doesn't require that it be on the face?

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: The statute does not, Your Honor, it is Regulation Z which requires --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That it be on the face.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And if Regulation Z had come out saying that some security interests have to be on the face and it's here ?

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: That's correct. I would note that the Federal Reserve Board has issued a series of rulings which have required disclosure of a wide range of security interests, security interests which do not fit into Ford Credit's characterization of essential security interests. The Federal Reserve Board, in their rulings set out at page 9 of our brief, have required disclosure of security interests in bank accounts, credit union accounts, and after interest is essential, but whether -- but merely that any security interest be required to be disclosed. The Regula­ tion Z definition, as I indicated, is any interest in property which secures payment or performance of an obligation and thus: a two-pronged test is set up by Regulation Z and every Court which has applied that test to the issue in this case has concluded that a creditor's claim to returned insurance premiums is a security interest under the Truth- in-Lending Act. Although the first test, the first prong of the Regulation Z definition, is any interest in property, Ford insurance premiums is significant: to Ford Credit, it has vision over the last 10 years by our estimate, an estimate Ford Credit has not disputed.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: When you say term insurance premiums, you're not talking about the life insurance premiums that -- they were talking about in 1970, are you?

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: This case involves physical damage insurance premiums, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Just what exactly is the property interest; you know, described in --

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: Well in this case, for example, it involved the physical damage insurance premium that exceeded $200. Given a cancellation of that insurance upon the default of the consumer, Ford Credit would have been en- t itled to that $215 by virtue of its clause. And in this case, it meant $215 to the consumer.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And why would Ford -- why would the insurance company have cancelled?

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: What happens is, is on default of the consumer the -- by virtue of the contractual assignment clause, Ford Credit is entitled to -- upon default, the insurance is cancelled because there's no longer any need for the continuation of physical damage insurance since the car has been repossessed, at that moment, Ford Credit is entitled to all returned insurance premiums that would exist at that time.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Alop, do you think the buyer reasonably would have expected to get the premium in that situation?

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: Well, consumers will have no means of having any opportunity to know that they are entitled to these funds unless the matter is meaningfully disclosed to them.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well they really would not be entitled to the funds if they are in default on the loan, would they?

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: They are entitled to -- without any clause, of course, those funds would go directly to the consumer, if Ford Credit --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well who holds the policy?

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: The policy is -- the insurance company issues- a policy directly to the consumer and without --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Doesn't there have to be a copy to the dealer or the finance company?

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: I do not know.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I mean, how does the finance company know that the car is being insured, that it's a contractual obligation to get insurance?

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: The contract requires the consumer to it fairly clear that whatever money comes back will be paid not expect that to happen?

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: The money of course, for example, in this case, the consumer purchased the insurance from an would tend to think that that money would come back to the consumer, given default or given a cancellation of the insur­ ance policy. But by virtue of Ford Credit's --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Even though he was in default on his loan he would think that?

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: There would be no reason for the money to go from the insurance company to the dealer, to Ford Credit, absent the clause in the --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Oh, I understand that, but wouldn't the buyer of the car realize that that's why he's carrying the insurance, to protect the -- his ability to pay off the debt?

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: No, the insurance protects the auto­ mobile from collision.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Right! ■ is what you're saying?

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: I'm saying that --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It seems very unlikely to me. these refunds will be going to Ford Credit rather than to be coming back to the consumer, absent the meaningful dis­ closure on the front side of the contract. I was referring to the significance of the inter­ est to the creditor. It's -- Ford Credit has recovered substantial funds by virtue of this security interest over the last 10 years and this is an attractive --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But aren't those recoveries all against a larger, unpaid debt that they weren't able to collect? I mean, isn't that always when they get the money on these refunds ?

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: There are --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: They don't make any profit on it.

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: There are occasionally surpluses, in which the secured property, the automobile, for example, will -- the value of that will exceed the outstanding debt.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But then the buyer gets the surplus?

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: The buyer is supposed to get that surplus, the buyer will have no means of knowing that this money is coming to the buyer unless it is disclosed.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, but isn't it a fact that the 100 million or whatever the big sum of money is that Ford has Thus, Ford Credit's interest in this returned insurance prongs of the Regulation Z test for security interest are met. Contrary to the assertion of Ford Credit which was made today, the interest it retains in secured premiums would, if properly disclosed to consumers, be understood by consumers --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Incidentally, Mr. Alop, may I ask how

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: The total amount of the premium, in this case was $215.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Oh, and the return would be about what?

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: The return would of course depend at the time -- on the timing of the cancellation of the insur­ ance .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do we know here when it was?

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: No we do not, Your Honor. Ford Credit also has argued that --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: May I ask one other question as a matter of history, is it true that before this statute was passed it was customary for the finance companies to buy the insur­ ance themselves, and then they would buy the policy and make the purchaser pay the premiums? premiums. Under both. I don't think the historical analysis there would explain anything. Ford Credit argued that this information would create an information overload. Certainly when the matter is disclosed, as it is in the present contract, buried on the reverse side and clothed in the language of an assign­ ment clause, in one sentence which is 58 words long, it is confusing. However, the Third Circuit Court of Appeals in the Gennuso decision indicated that a creditor's security interest in returned insurance premiums could be meaningfully disclosed to consumers in a matter of a few words and also importantly, intelligibly and meaningfully. And we would note that Ford Credit's new contract which it has adopted subsequent to the decision below, does disclose this infor­ mation to consumers on the front side of the contract --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well the decision below told it it had to ?

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's no-- not very surprising.

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: My point is however, that they have, they are now disclosing this information concisely in a matter of five words on the front side of the contract; that's hardly creating an information overload that they refer to. I would also note that Ford Credit's reliance on the Truth-in-Lending Simplification and Reform Act is mis­ placed. That Act is not effective until 1982, and -- it is not retroactive, it is not made applicable to pending litigation or is it applicable -- or made retroactively applicable, thus. : by it's own terms, the Simplification Act is not relevant to this case and by virtue of 1 United States Code Section 109, Ford Credit's liability for viola­ tions of the existing Truth-in-Lending Act is not extin­ guished by virtue of the enactment of the Simplification Act. Code Section 109?

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: Section 1 U. S. Code Section 109 have --

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: That's correct. transaction. The only thing Ford Credit has relied on, since there is nothing in the language of the Act or in the Committee Reports that underlie it, the only thing Ford Credit has relied on is a statement, an isolated statement of one senator -- Senator Garn, to the effect, arguing that note, was not a member of the body that enacted the Truth- in-Lending Act, the Congress that enacted the Truth-in-Lend­ ing Act, and Senator Garn's statement is not reflected in the Committee Report; instead, it was inserted in the Con­ gressional Record after the Committee Report was issued and it's not reflected in the Committee Report.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And his statement was to the effect that the new provisions of the Simplification Act were simply clarifying?

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: That's correct. However this Court in the past, has given little if any, weight to the post-passage remarks of legislators regarding the meaning of previously enacted legislation, particularly where the legislator was not a member of the enacting body. the decision in this case should be made prospective only, with regard -- except as with regard to the Respondents in this case. However, by placing the argument in this posture, by conceding that the Respondent should recover, regardless of the application of the decision as given, Ford Credit has destroyed any adversarial relationship between the parties on this issue. That is, Ford Credit has presented no case or controversy to this Court on the issue of the application this decision is to be given. Ford Credit on versy in that it seeks no relief that will affect the Respondents in any way, it seeks no alteration in the judg­ ment below, and as a consequence, in essence it seeks an advisory opinion that will affect other litigation, pending litigation in which the Respondents have no interest.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The Court announced a decision this morning with which I am sure you haven't yet had time to familiarize yourself -- Kirchberg v. Feenstra, which might have some bearing on this aspect of the case.

turns.name: ALOP
turns.text: As it is our opinion that the Court need not reach the issue of prospective application, we will stand on our briefs with regard to the Chevron analysis. Thank you, Your Honor.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Do you have anything

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: One minute left, Your Honor?

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: One.

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: Thank you. ORAL REBUTTAL ARGUMENT OF AARON J. KRAMER, ESQ.,

turns.name: KRAMER
turns.text: The purpose of the clause that we've North American Reporting hereby certifies that the attached pages represent an accurate transcript of electronic sound recording of the oral argument before the Supreme Court of the United States in the matter of: No. 80-84 proceedings for the records of the Court. BY: UL
